さらに、[[神田明神]]末社の籠祖神社（現・合祀殿）では、塩土翁神が山幸彦のために無目籠（まなしかたま、隙間なく編んだカゴ）を作ったという伝説から、竹細工職人たちが塩土翁神を自分たちの祖神として信仰したという例もある。
鹽竈神社の末社の1社である[[御釜神社]]には、鹽竈神社と同じく塩土老翁神が祀られているが、御釜神社にある「四口の神釜（よんくのしんかま）」のうち、向かって右手前にある、他より一回り小さい釜は「御台の釜（おだいのかま）」と呼ばれ、塩土老翁神が特に愛用した釜であると伝わっている。
先に述べた通り、江戸時代には塩土老翁神は猿田彦、事勝国勝、岐神、興玉命、太田命と同体異名の神とされたことがあったが、特に[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦神]]との関係性について、鹽竈神社末社の[[鼻節神社]]（[[宮城県]][[七ヶ浜町]]）の祭神が猿田彦神であること、また神田明神末社の籠祖神社（現・合祀殿）では猿田彦大神と鹽土翁神が共に祀られていることなどから、塩土老翁神は'''猿田彦神'''と関係の深い神であるということが指摘される。
鹽竈神社宮司であった押木耿介は著者『鹽竈神社』の中で、志波彦神社に祀られる志波彦神は、塩土老翁神の幸御魂であるという人もいるという説を紹介した上で、今後の研究を待たねばならないとしている<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=鹽竈神社 |date=昭和47年8月1日初刷発行 |publisher=学生社 |page=187}}</ref>。
塩竈市をはじめ宮城県の銘菓として知られる「[[志ほがま]]」は、 塩土老翁神が藻塩草より採取した塩と米粉とを混ぜて作った糧食を起源とするいう言い伝えがある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.baikado.com/%E6%A2%85%E8%8A%B1%E5%A0%82/%E5%95%86%E5%93%81%E4%B8%80%E8%A6%A7/ |title=梅花堂 商品一覧 「志ほがま」 |access-date=2023-05-16}}</ref>。
== 日本の神社一覧 == === 塩竈神社及び塩竈神社からの勧請社 === * [[鹽竈神社]]（[[宮城県]][[塩竈市]]）- 総本宮 * [[塩竈神社_(曖昧さ回避)]]（全国各地） * [[鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)|鹽竈神社（東京都港区）]] * [[高室神社]]（[[静岡県]][[掛川市]]） * [[六所神社_(岡崎市)]]（[[愛知県]][[岡崎市]]） * [[六所神社_(豊田市)]]（愛知県[[豊田市]]） * [[塩竈六社大明神]]（[[和歌山県]][[田辺市]]）
=== その他の神社 === * [[青島神社]] ([[宮崎県]][[宮崎市]]) * [[船魂神社]] ([[北海道]][[函館市]]) *[[籠祖神社]]（東京都[[千代田区]]鎮座[[神田神社]]境内社<ref>現在は合祀殿に合祀されている。</ref>） * [[白髯神社]]（[[福井県]][[あわら市]]） * [[志宝屋神社]]（[[三重県]][[伊勢市]]:[[豊受大神宮]]（外宮）末社） * [[塩屋神社]]（[[広島県]][[広島市]]） * [[蔵前神社]]<ref>創建当初から境内神社として鎮座する鹽竈神社（陸前国宮城郡鎮座鹽竈神社遥拝殿）を平成7年に合祀したもので、当社では塩土翁命（しおつちのおきなのみこと）という神名を用いている。</ref>（東京都[[台東区]]）
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の神の一覧]] * [[オオヤマツミ]] * [[海幸山幸]] * [[ニニギ|瓊々杵尊]] * [[住吉三神]]

revision.text:
'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}

Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}

revision.text:
'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}

== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

[[ja:塩土老翁]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

[[ja:塩土老翁]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}

[[ja:塩土老翁]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."

His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.

[[ja:塩土老翁]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}

[[ja:塩土老翁]]

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing page creation to myself as per first edit summary
revision.text:
'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}

[[ja:塩土老翁]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}

His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

revision.text:
'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}

[[ja:塩土老翁]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

[[ja:塩土老翁]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

[[ja:塩土老翁]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

[[ja:塩土老翁]] [[Category:New]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}

The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

[[ja:塩土老翁]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

[[ja:塩土老翁]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

[[ja:塩土老翁]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

[[ja:塩土老翁]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiotsuchi-no-Oji]][[Category:qq]]

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)

revision.text:
'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}

Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}

'''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' (塩土老翁) is a {{ill|kami|ja|神 (神道)}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}.
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is referred to as '''Shiotsuchi no Kami''' (塩椎神), while in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he appears as '''Shiotsuchi no Oji''' or '''Shiotsu no Oji''' (塩土老翁/塩筒老翁). Additionally, the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} also mentions him as Shiotsuchi no Oji. Variations of his name include "塩土老翁神" and "塩土翁神," with the character for "塩" sometimes written in its archaic form "鹽." Pronunciations include "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami" or "Shiotsuchi no Oji no Kami."
== Overview == The name "Shiotsuchi" can be interpreted as "Master of the Tides" or "Way of the Tides," associating the deity with control over tidal currents and navigation. In {{ill|Kiki mythology|ja|記紀神話}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji plays a critical role, offering guidance and information to key figures. His portrayal as a coastal deity who imparts wisdom has parallels to the "Old Man of the Sea" motif found in {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシア神話}}, such as {{ill|Proteus|ja|プローテウス}}. Additionally, he is revered as a god of salt production.
The shrine tradition of {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in {{ill|Shiogama|ja|塩竈市}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, regards him as the deity who guided {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|フツヌシ}} during their pacification of the land. Shiotsuchi no Oji remained in the area to teach fishing and salt production to the people, making him a prominent deity in these domains.
== Legends == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}’s account of the {{ill|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson|ja|天孫降臨}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji is identified as {{ill|Kotosakukuni Katsunagasa|ja|事勝国勝長狭神}}. He offers his land to {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} upon his arrival at Kasasa Cape. Another tradition identifies him as a son of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}.
In the myth of {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|山幸彦と海幸彦}}, Shiotsuchi no Oji assists {{ill|Hoori|ja|ホオリ}}, who had lost his brother's fishing hook. He provides a small boat (or tightly woven basket) and instructs Hoori to let the tides guide him to the palace of the sea deity {{ill|Watatsumi|ja|ワタツミ}}.
During the {{ill|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武東征}}, the Nihon Shoki recounts that Shiotsuchi no Oji inspired Emperor {{ill|Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to head east, stating, "There is good land to the east."
== Shrines == Shiotsuchi no Oji is enshrined at notable shrines, including: * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} (Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture) – The main shrine. * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine (Tokyo Minato)|ja|鹽竈神社 (東京都港区)}} (Tokyo) * {{ill|Takamu Shrine|ja|高室神社}} (Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Okazaki)|ja|六所神社 (岡崎市)}} (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Rokusho Shrine (Toyota)|ja|六所神社 (豊田市)}} (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture) * {{ill|Shioya Shrine (Hiroshima)|ja|塩屋神社}} (Hiroshima City, Hiroshima Prefecture)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Oyamatsumi|ja|オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Umisachi and Yamasachi|ja|海幸山幸}} * {{ill|Ninigi|ja|ニニギ}} * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}}
